content.problemStatement:
Let BBB be a **binary** string of length NNN.

The integer XXX is said to be a witness for BBB if the following condition holds:


Consider two arrays S1S_1S1‚Äč and S2S_2S2‚Äč, initially both empty.
For each index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N),

If Bi=0B_i = 0Bi‚Äč=0, append (X & i)(X \ \& \ i)(X & i) to S1S_1S1‚Äč.

Here, &\&& represents the bitwise AND operation.
If Bi=1B_i = 1Bi‚Äč=1, append (X‚ą£i)(X \mid i)(X‚ą£i) to S2S_2S2‚Äč.

Here, ‚ą£\mid‚ą£ represents the bitwise OR operation.


In the end, if S1‚ą©S2=‚ąÖS_1 \cap S_2 = \emptysetS1‚Äč‚ą©S2‚Äč=‚ąÖ holds, i.e. S1S_1S1‚Äč and S2S_2S2‚Äč have no common elements, then XXX is said to be a witness for BBB.
For example, consider B=010B = \texttt{010}B=010.


X=1X = 1X=1 is a witness for this string, because S1=[1 & 1,1 & 3]=[1,1]S_1 = [1\ \&\ 1, 1\ \&\ 3] = [1, 1]S1‚Äč=[1 & 1,1 & 3]=[1,1], and S2=[1‚ą£2]=[3]S_2 = [1\mid 2] = [3]S2‚Äč=[1‚ą£2]=[3] are disjoint.
X=2X = 2X=2 is not a witness for this string, because S1=[2 & 1,2 & 3]=[0,2]S_1 = [2\ \&\ 1, 2\ \&\ 3] = [0, 2]S1‚Äč=[2 & 1,2 & 3]=[0,2], and S2=[2‚ą£2]=[2]S_2 = [2\mid 2] = [2]S2‚Äč=[2‚ą£2]=[2] are not disjoint (they both contain the element 222).
You are given a binary string AAA of length NNN.

In one operation, you can choose an index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N), and flip the character at index iii, i.e. convert it from 000 to 111 or vice versa.

Find the minimum number of operations needed to convert AAA into a binary string such that **no integer** from 111 to NNN is a witness to it.

Note that we do not care whether integers larger than NNN are witnesses or not - just that no integer between 111 and NNN is a witness.

If it's impossible to convert AAA to such a binary string no matter what, print ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the binary string.
The second line contains the binary string AAA of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, print the answer on a new line: the minimum number of changes needed to obtain a binary string that admits no witnesses in [1,N][1, N][1,N], or ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 if it's impossible.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all tests won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For A=010A = \texttt{010}A=010, X=1X = 1X=1 is a witness: we obtain S1=[1,1]S_1 = [1, 1]S1‚Äč=[1,1] and S2=[3]S_2 = [3]S2‚Äč=[3], which are disjoint.
We can use an operation on i=1i = 1i=1 to change A1A_1A1‚Äč to 111, obtaining A=110A = \texttt{110}A=110.
It can be verified that AAA now has no witnesses in [1,3][1, 3][1,3].

For X=1X = 1X=1, the arrays are S1=[1]S_1 = [1]S1‚Äč=[1] and S2=[1,3]S_2 = [1, 3]S2‚Äč=[1,3], which are not disjoint.
For X=2X = 2X=2, the arrays are S1=[2]S_1 = [2]S1‚Äč=[2] and S2=[3,2]S_2 = [3, 2]S2‚Äč=[3,2], which are not disjoint.
For X=3X = 3X=3, the arrays are S1=[3]S_1 = [3]S1‚Äč=[3] and S2=[3,3]S_2 = [3, 3]S2‚Äč=[3,3], which are not disjoint.

Test case 222: It can be proved that every binary string of length 444 has at least one integer in [1,4][1, 4][1,4] be a witness, so no solution exists.
Test case 333: One optimal solution is to turn the string into A=11010A = \texttt{11010}A=11010 by operating on indices 111 and 555.
